turns.content: Why does this even work this way where I have to type out all the escape characters for newlines in a string literal instead of just using a normal newline? It feels like I'm fighting the compiler on every line.

That frustration is very common when transitioning from Python or JavaScript because those languages handle escapes more gracefully. In Rust, you use raw string literals with the r prefix to avoid escaping entirely. For example, writing r#"Line 1\nLine 2"# still requires a backslash, but using just "Line 1
Line 2" will cause a syntax error because newlines are not allowed inside regular double-quoted strings in Rust. The raw literal syntax is defined by the core language and allows you to include actual line breaks directly in your source code text.

Ah okay, so if I just want to avoid typing backslashes for SQL queries or regex patterns, can I actually use that? It seems like it would be much cleaner than manually escaping every special character.

Yes, raw string literals are specifically designed for this scenario. You can write r#"SELECT * FROM users WHERE age > 18;"# without any backslashes, making it readable and maintainable. This is particularly useful when writing complex regex patterns because you don't need to escape dots or backslashes manually. However, I want to clarify something before we continue: are you planning to use these raw strings for SQL queries primarily, or do you also intend to parse them with the string module?

I'm mostly using it for SQL and regex right now since those have so many special characters that escaping is painful. But I am aware of the string module later on too. So basically, raw strings are just a way to skip the escaping hassle in Rust?

Exactly, you can think of them as a shortcut where the compiler skips the escape processing step for you entirely. It's like telling the system "I promise this text is safe and doesn't need any special handling." There is an analogy here: imagine writing a recipe where every time you want to say 'a cup' you have to write 'a\s\c\u\p'. Using raw strings lets you just write 'a cup' directly. You can now use r#"..."# syntax to write your SQL and regex without fighting backslashes.
